learning_outcomes.description: Understand the quantum formalism used to describe the properties of atoms and molecules.
Calculate features of atomic and molecular systems such as emission spectra, energy level structure and allowed transitions.
Describe and apply both semiclassical and quantum theories of atom-light interaction, recognising their respective domains of applicability.
Recognise and quantitatively describe the kinds of optical phenomena that classical theories of light cannot predict.
Understand how the electromagnetic field can be quantized, and be able to describe mathematically some important quantum states of light.
Use the formalism of quantum optics to solve problems relating to the manipulation of the quantum states of light.
Describe the typical kinds of optical elements used in quantum optics experiments and their role in manipulating the state of light.

Understand the application of atomic and molecular physics as well as quantum optics to some important current research endeavours, such as high precision metrology, quantum information, and tests of fundamental physics.

assessments.task_description:
The lecturer will nominate selected problems as homework. There are 4 assignments in total, equally weighted. Students should submit their solutions to these problems by the due dates.

The applied classes are largely formative, rather than summative, assessment. In other words, they are important opportunities for learning and obtaining feedback on your progress, rather than tests of what you already know.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Homework problems must be submitted electronically via Blackboard.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.due_date: Seminars will be scheduled during regular lecture times between Week 10 and Week 11 inclusive. Dates will be given in the first week of the course.
assessments.task_description:
Each student will deliver a 10-minute seminar to the class on a topic related to the course (8-minute talk and 2 minutes of questions). We will follow a "conference style" format consisting of back-to-back seminars. 

We will upload a list of topics for you to choose, but you may also suggest your own. In this case, it's advisable to talk to the lecturers to make sure your seminar will be adequate for the level and content of this course.  

The presentations will be in-person.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description: The end-of-semester exam will be an in-person, paper-based exam. Students will sit the exam at the St Lucia Campus.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 1 To Week 13(27 Jul - 01 Nov)
learning_activities.topic:
Lectures

Lectures will provide a broad overview of the course material and will provide a forum in which to discuss questions and issues arising from the readings.
learning_activities.topic_description: Lectures Lectures will provide a broad overview of the course material and will provide a forum in which to discuss questions and issues arising from the readings.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 1 To Week 13(27 Jul - 01 Nov)
learning_activities.topic:
Applied Classes

In applied classes, students will put into practice the methods and techniques taught in the course to solve problems in quantum optics and atomic physics. Problem-solving will be guided by lecturers. These classes will aid in the development of problem-solving techniques and consolidation of course material.
